ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for San Pellegrino branded sparkling mineral water; FOODON_03301099 (carbonated water) is the closest parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bottle of San Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water is 750 ml, equivalent to approximately 750 g, typically shared at the table or consumed individually over a meal.

nutrient_source.title: S.Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water — Nutritional Information (per 100 ml)
nutrient_source.organization: Sanpellegrino Group / USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (NDB No. 45347023)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for S.Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water confirms 0 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml. Sodium reported at approximately 11.6 mg per 100 ml per manufacturer label data cross-referenced with USDA entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; convention for non-glycemic beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain water and sparkling mineral water contain no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore have no measurable glycemic index. By established convention in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature, such beverages are assigned GI = 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) with gi_category = low. Glycemic load per 750 ml serving = 0.0 because carbohydrate_g = 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (11.6 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: San Pellegrino Sparkling Mineral Water contains approximately 11.6 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is well within the low-sodium threshold; individuals with hypertension can consume it freely as part of adequate daily fluid intake without meaningful impact on blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.guidance: San Pellegrino Sparkling Mineral Water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it a safe and recommended hydration choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes without any impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium; natural mineral content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: San Pellegrino Sparkling Mineral Water is free of fat, cholesterol, and added sugars, and its naturally occurring calcium (16.3 mg/100 ml) and magnesium (5.3 mg/100 ml) content may contribute modestly to cardiovascular mineral intake; it is a heart-healthy hydration option for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: San Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water is a premium Italian carbonated mineral water sourced from the San Pellegrino Terme spring in the Bergamo province of Lombardy, Italy. The water is naturally rich in minerals including calcium (approximately 163 mg/L), magnesium (approximately 53 mg/L), and sodium (approximately 116 mg/L), and is lightly carbonated with natural carbon dioxide. It contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates, making it entirely non-glycemic and suitable for all dietary patterns including vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. The water has a distinctive fine effervescence and a clean, slightly mineral taste that pairs well with food, particularly Italian cuisine. It is commonly served in 750 ml glass bottles in restaurants. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its absence of sugars, fat, and cholesterol, and its low sodium content. It is one of the world's most recognised sparkling mineral water brands and is widely available in Singapore including Italian dining establishments.

meal_context: accompaniment to pasta and pizza meals

Serving size reflects the 750 ml restaurant bottle as described in the field survey source record from Ricciotti - The Riverwalk. This is an Italian branded product, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes do not apply.
